text.title: AR Design Patterns Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules anchoring the ar-design-patterns methodology, each grounded in a source.

rule.statement: Virtual content MUST be anchored to a detected plane or saved anchor; world-locked content without anchoring is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Unanchored content drifts as the SLAM map refines; users perceive instability.

rule.statement: Interactive UI placed in world space MUST respect real-world occlusion (people occlusion + scene depth where available).
rule.rationale: UI floating in front of occluding real objects breaks presence and creates depth-perception errors.

rule.statement: Cross-platform AR apps MUST define a single gesture grammar (one-finger drag = move, pinch = scale, two-finger rotate = rotate) and respect platform-native shortcuts.
rule.rationale: Inconsistent gestures across platforms confuse users who switch devices; platform conventions reduce learning cost.

rule.statement: Materials on placed objects MUST use the runtime's lighting estimation; flat-shaded materials in a lit room break realism and accessibility (low contrast against real surfaces).
rule.rationale: Without lighting estimation, virtual objects appear pasted-on; matte materials lose definition.

rule.statement: On track-loss event the app MUST surface a reset/relocalise prompt with the world-state preserved; silent reset is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Silent loss creates floating UI and lost work; user-visible reset prompt with state preservation maintains trust.

rule.statement: If the Skip-If preconditions hold, the methodology MUST be skipped and the caller routed to the linked alternative.
rule.rationale: Applying this machinery to the wrong workflow burns tokens without producing a usable artefact.
